_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal upward slope indicating higher yields for longer maturities due to risk and time preferences. An inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, is historically associated with impending recessions typically 6-18 months ahead. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.57%, and the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread is positive at 0.37 points, indicating a normal upward slope. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted (spread below 0), marking one of the deepest inversions at zero points, before re-steepening recently.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a key recession signal because inversions have preceded every modern US recession. After spending 2022-2024 inverted, the spread has now re-steepened above zero to 0.37 points, suggesting a reduction in near-term recession risks. However, an important caveat is that the curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession starts, so re-steepening does not rule out a later downturn. The New York Fed assigns a 31% probability of recession based on current data, indicating a moderate recession risk consistent with the recent curve normalization.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's positive 0.37 point 2s10s spread combined with the 4.57% 10-year yield signals a return to a normal upward slope after inversion. While this reduces the immediate recession warning from the curve, the NY Fed's 31% recession probability suggests that risks remain tangible. Overall, the curve signals caution but not an imminent downturn based on its shape and the Fed's statistical outlook.
